author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: I thought a light box was a box that you have even lighting from all sides of an item you want to photograph? if I am wrong on that being what you are describing here then there is your next project should you want one, LOL I have one I made with 5 dollars worth of fluorescent tube LED replacements (these were like 2.50 each in a box of like 16 bulbs, but are also the type that use the ballast still attached vs the ones that you remove and wire the 120v (here in USA) directly to the connectors on the bulb and thus (I fixed one of those types of bulbs that had a bad cap so super easy to fix and then hooked up wires directly to the thing going to a plug, and encased it in a custom C channel of aluminum so I can hang it anywhere as a single stand alone bulb that is 30w per bulb too, (the 2.50 units are all only 15w each giving about 180% of the light per fixture than they did with the best regular fluorescent bulbs that used to go in the fixture at either 32watts each or 40 watts each reducing power by over 50% and increasing light by nearly 200% XD )

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text:
yeah that is what we call a backlighted tracing table LOL (it also works well for placing adhesive on the back of something you have on paper so you can be sure it is lined up with the item on the other side, which I do with transfer tape that has no body its all just rubber like adhesive, on paper after I laser print labels for shipping, so I am not wasting any of it on those labels, (I* have some transfer tape too that has a body that is white on one side and frosted aluminum metallic on the other, which I some times use right on the section of the barcode to ensure that scanning will work properly when it is passing through the mail system :) ) 

I have two I made from LCD and old neon tube based flat panel screens, one is whole with me plugging into the wall and running it as is with no image just white light, and the other is just the clear plastic thick panel that the light is shining through from the edge to light up your LCD screen but I used RGB LEDs on a strip (5050 type) that I actually use the color change ability to my advantage since I can have white lighting dimmed down so I can tell which direction the light is coming from be it inside the panel, and under the paper, or from behind me off of the lamps around me, :) just look up Tesla temporal Hour reserve bill design on youtube to see what I used it for :)

author: @NorthernKitty
text: Wow, those are inexpensive!!  That's a fantastic solution for a light box, and would probably work well as a softbox for close-up video or photography.  I'm not aware of anyone selling those Garrisons or anything similar in the U.S. for that price.  (Which makes me think, there's a market waiting to be filled here!!)  I've made my own 21" panel by gutting an LCD monitor and lining it with strips of LED's, much like you suggested Luke will be showing us soon.  Comprised of about 60 LEDs, mine runs off of just 4 watts but is enough to light my parlor.  I've been hoping to get my hands on some trashed monitors so I can make some more of these panels.  By the way, I'm curious as to what voltage DC that transformer converts the AC to?  With the right cable, you could probably plug that straight into a small solar generator and run it for much the day.  😊  The box you built is big and sturdy enough that you could probably place a small 2 lbs lithium scooter battery in there and run it for 2 hours.  😀

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think it is 24V and yes you could for certain - I also agree there is a market here should someone want to pursue it

author: @sofa-lofa4241
text:
If you are ordering Led's for this project bear in mind the colour temperature,
If you get some in the range of 2000 -  3500K then you may find it is too 'warm and bit 'orangey'
If they are above 5000K or so then they can look very cold and harsh with too much blue,

I find that around 4500 - 5000K is what I prefer for work lights, it is sometimes described by sellers as natural or daylight white, you may have different preferences/needs, such as video/photo light, S. A. D. lamp, etc. 
It is always worth checking out what colour temperature the expensive pro units use and getting something fairly close if you can

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I wouldn't order LEDs mate - just buy the light panel - this is meant to be super easy - an hour or two in the shop and a nice but inexpensive present comparable to a store bought gift - that's the idea

replies.author: @sofa-lofa4241
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkeringyeah, they are a good price but I find the panels are more difficult to find in different colour temperatures, there seem to be about 2 or 3 common ones but I haven't seen much else, I find there is much more choice with the strips/flexi-tape, 

The point I was trying to make about colour temperature is that for any specific use there is a range for that,
For example, if someone wanted a video light then 2700K or 6500K would probably look horrible and the recipient of the present would say 'Thanks, that's great!' and then quickly ditch it and pay £100 for one that actually works,

I get that it is something you put together with stuff to hand in a very short time and it works great for the intended use, but with a bit of thought/research for about 10 minutes then you can build the perfect light box for your application, and I find the strips/flexi-tape is usually the easiest way to go for custom applications

author: @jasonwitt8619
text: Oh WOW mate, I like it. I need one for sure. Here is a tip, I sometimes like to burn a pattern or picture into wood. I use standard carbon tracing paper, it is the same size as a piece of printer paper then just lay the carbon paper down and then lay the picture over it, next put 4 thumb tacks in the corners to hold it down. Begin to trace the picture with a bright red pen so that you can see what you have already traced. If you push down hard enough, it even looks burned already and can be ready for a clear lacquer finish like the poly 3 in 1 that you and I have talked about before. Cheers mate and tell all your artist friends, they will love how easy this is to do. Oh and you can buys the paper in even large sheets for real cheap if needed.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is an awesome tip mate - very nice share thanks for that - I might even do a vid to get a wider share of that one if that's ok with you?

replies.author: @jasonwitt8619
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Sure mate, that would be awesome

author: @amb3cog
text: It would be great if you showed how to make one of these designed specifically for people with SAD (Seasonal Affective Disorder). That would be a great gift too. This might even work too? I don't know much about it, other then seeing them on 60 Minutes one time. And seeing them for sale on Amazon. Either way. Great idea/project/video as usual. God Bless. ✌️

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: These would be great for that purpose!!  All you'd need to do is make sure you order a "daylight" or "full spectrum" LED panel, which would be in the 5000 Kelvin to 6000 Kelvin color temperature range.  To combat SAD, you need about 10,000 lux of daylight for around 30 minutes, and I've no doubt that was putting out enough lux!!  😋

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: just use a daylight simulating LED panel mate - you can buy them - the do the same thing

replies.author: @amb3cog
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Very cool. I've talked to people with this disorder, and explained to them about buying one of these. As they're not outrageously expensive, especially compared to what they used to be a few decades ago. But I'm sure they'll be a lot more receptive to the idea of putting a cheap one together themselves, over paying 4 times as much from Amazon. Thank you very much. ✌️

replies.author: @sofa-lofa4241
replies.text:
A quick Internet search will give you all the info you need to build a S. A. D. panel yourself,
I would look at what colour temperature/lumens the commercial ones are using and find a source of led's that are fairly accurate, I don't think Cats Pajamas is far off with 5000K - 6000K
I found good info in a photo/video diy forum, some people there had the equipment to check the colour temperature/C. R. I., etc. and were able to point people to cheap but correctly rated led's,

I have seen a couple of DIY panels where 3 or 4 strips are used of different colour temperatures, each strip controlled by a pot near the handle... Mix your own light!
